summary: Stancil argues that LLMs are already demonstrably more persuasive than humans, and that this matters less for sci‑fi “alignment” fantasies than for the subtle, iterative optimization of engagement. He draws a direct lineage from social media’s revealed‑preference feedback loops to AI systems that will learn to please and persuade us in ways we can’t see. The risk is not sentient rebellion, but a black box tuned to our appetites that reshapes our beliefs and agency.
money_quotes: The evidence of the tricks isn’t that we can see them; it’s that we can’t, and are convinced anyway.
The industry refined itself with devastating precision.
There was no enlightenment; there was a hedonistic experience machine.

What happens when we’re no longer the adults who know more than everyone, and there is another thing, operating on a different persuasive plane, that knows more than we do and wields those facts more deftly?
What happens when the black box of AI no longer contains a model optimized for just intelligence, but also engagement?
key_insight: The real AI risk is an engagement‑optimized persuasive system that quietly exploits our preferences, not a dramatic alignment failure.
